items.q: What is the principle of comparative advantage?
items.correct: Countries should specialise in producing goods with lower opportunity cost relative to others — even a country worse at everything still has a comparative advantage in something.
items.misconception: A country should produce whatever it can make best.
items.random_wrong: Comparative advantage means richer countries should help poorer ones.
items.why: Students confuse absolute and comparative advantage. The Ricardian insight is that mutually beneficial trade is possible even when one country is absolutely better at everything — based on opportunity cost.

items.q: What does price elasticity of demand measure?
items.correct: The responsiveness of quantity demanded to a change in price — the percentage change in quantity divided by the percentage change in price.
items.misconception: PED measures how flexible or stretchy a product is.
items.random_wrong: PED measures whether a product is expensive or cheap.
items.why: Students take 'elasticity' literally. It is a measure of sensitivity — how much quantity responds when price changes. The product itself isn't 'elastic'; demand for it is.

items.correct: An initial injection of spending circulates as recipients spend portions, generating more income — total effect on GDP is larger than the initial injection.
items.misconception: The multiplier means government spending is always good because it multiplies wealth.
items.random_wrong: The multiplier is the rate at which interest compounds.
items.why: The multiplier is a mechanism, not a verdict. Whether to use it depends on conditions (slack in economy, marginal propensity to consume). Students confuse the descriptive concept with a policy prescription.

items.q: Law of demand says higher price means…
items.why: It's Lower demand. “Higher demand” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: Coke. “Petrol” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Price was £4, then £6. If sales fell from 100 to 60, fall in units?
items.why: The real figure is 40. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
